summary: Gruber addresses Apple's rejection of the HKmap Live app, which helped Hong Kong protesters track police locations. He applies Hanlon's Razor, suggesting the rejection was likely a bad call by a rank-and-file App Store reviewer rather than deliberate kowtowing to China. However, he argues that given the importance of Hong Kong's democracy struggle and Apple's deep financial ties to China, Apple must apply extra scrutiny to avoid even the appearance of siding with Beijing. The post updates with good news that Apple reversed the decision and approved the app.
key_points: Apple rejected the HKmap Live app citing it helped users evade law enforcement, drawing comparisons to apps like Waze that do similar things
Gruber invokes Hanlon's Razor, suggesting incompetence rather than malice likely explains the rejection
Apple's dual dependence on China — manufacturing and sales roughly equal to all of Europe — creates a perception problem that demands heightened scrutiny
The stakes are uniquely high because Hong Kong represents a liberal democracy standing up to an authoritarian superpower
Apple ultimately reversed course and approved the app
money_quotes: Hanlon's Razor — 'Never attribute to malice that which is adequately explained by stupidity' — has never applied to anything more aptly than App Store rejections.
What's going on in Hong Kong is important. A small liberal democracy is standing up to a gargantuan authoritarian communist dictatorship with a superpower-grade military force.
If Apple wants to avoid any suspicion that the company is kowtowing to China, they need to avoid any inadvertent screw-ups in a case like this.
Everything related to the App Store approval process that might be perceived as kowtowing to China should receive the utmost scrutiny.
This one doesn't pass the test.
To deny the people of Hong Kong one of the few tools that defends them against police aggression is such a craven act that I can't even put it into words.
key_insight: Even if Apple's rejection of the Hong Kong protest app was mere incompetence rather than political capitulation, Apple's deep financial dependence on China means it cannot afford such careless mistakes on matters this consequential.
